summary: Muratori questions why modern CPUs still use register-based instruction encodings when compilers and processors have both evolved past needing them literally. He argues that compilers naturally think in terms of memory operations and only deal with registers as an imposed constraint, while modern CPUs internally treat registers as abstract logical values anyway through out-of-order execution and register renaming. He proposes a stack-machine encoding where the top of the stack serves as a virtual register file, which could simplify context switching to saving just 3-4 values and remove artificial obfuscation between compiler and CPU. He acknowledges he may be missing something fundamental about hardware design and genuinely solicits answers from hardware engineers.
key_points: Compilers naturally reason about memory operations and only convert to register-based encodings because CPUs require it, adding unnecessary complexity to the compilation process

Modern CPUs already treat registers as abstract logical values through out-of-order execution and register renaming, so the register paradigm is a semantic fiction maintained at the instruction encoding level
A stack-relative addressing mode with compressed encodings could match register-based instruction sizes while eliminating the register abstraction entirely
Thread context switching would become trivially cheap — saving 3-4 values instead of the entire register file — if registers were just memory locations at the top of the stack
The real constraint may be that allowing registers to be addressable by pointers would create pipeline hazards, but this could be handled similarly to branch mispredictions
money_quotes: Compilers only care about registers because they have to.

By adding the constraint that most operations have to go through registers to happen, the current paradigm effectively adds a bunch of obfuscation from the compiler to the CPU, making it harder for the CPU to tell what is actually necessary.
When you talk about a 'register' in a modern CPU, you're really talking about a 'unique transient value' anyway!
A 'register' is now a semantic idea first, and an actual physical thing second.
The compiler knows what all the unique values are, and where they are in memory. The CPU needs to know that, too, and just wants to do the right series of operations.

If all register-esque operations were instead encoded as memory operations at the top of the stack, swapping threads would always be a matter of saving and restoring just 3 64-bit values, no matter how complex the processor's logic.
I asked some engineers at Intel the innocent question, 'Why is memory latency so high?' The answer was simply that the memory was literally far away from the CPU.
key_insight: The register abstraction in modern CPUs may be an anachronistic encoding convention that adds complexity for both compilers and processors, since both sides already reason about values abstractly rather than in terms of physical register locations.
